name: caching
context: Implement key-value caching with TTL, eviction policies, and cache patterns (aside, through, ahead)
info: This skill covers Redis caching operations: setting and getting key-value pairs, configuring TTL (time-to-live) for automatic expiry, choosing eviction policies (LRU, LFU, volatile-ttl, noeviction), and implementing cache patterns including cache-aside (lazy loading), cache-through (write-through), and cache-ahead (write-behind). The ioredis client provides pipelining for batch operations and Lua scripting for atomic cache updates. Proper cache invalidation strategies prevent stale data while maintaining high hit rates.
triggers: set a redis key with ttl

sections.title: Get and set operations
sections.body: Use `SET key value` and `GET key` for basic caching. `SET` supports flags: `EX` (seconds TTL), `PX` (milliseconds TTL), `NX` (only if not exists), `XX` (only if exists). Use `MGET` and `MSET` for batch operations. Serialize complex objects with `JSON.stringify`/`JSON.parse`. See [[redis#data-model]] for supported value types.

sections.title: TTL and expiry
sections.body: Set TTL at write time with `SET key value EX seconds` or after with `EXPIRE key seconds`. Check remaining TTL with `TTL key` (seconds) or `PTTL key` (milliseconds). Returns -1 if no expiry, -2 if key missing. Remove expiry with `PERSIST key`. Set absolute expiry with `EXPIREAT key timestamp`.

sections.title: Eviction policies
sections.body: Configure `maxmemory` and `maxmemory-policy` in redis.conf or at runtime. `allkeys-lru` is the best default for caching workloads. `allkeys-lfu` handles skewed access patterns better. `volatile-lru` and `volatile-ttl` only evict keys with TTL set, preserving persistent keys. `noeviction` returns errors when memory is full. See [[redis/clustering#cluster-mode]] for per-node memory configuration in cluster setups.

sections.title: Cache patterns
sections.body: Cache-aside (lazy loading): check cache first, load from source on miss, populate cache. Cache-through (write-through): write to cache and source simultaneously, ensures consistency. Cache-ahead (write-behind): write to cache immediately, flush to source asynchronously via a queue, higher throughput but eventual consistency. Choose based on read/write ratio and consistency requirements.

sections.title: Cache invalidation
sections.body: TTL-based invalidation is simplest: keys expire automatically. Event-driven invalidation uses [[redis/pub-sub]] to notify cache holders of changes. Tag-based invalidation groups related keys for bulk deletion. Version-based invalidation increments a version counter in the key name. Avoid using `KEYS` in production (blocks the server); use `SCAN` for pattern matching instead.
